refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall establish and maintain teleoperation communication channels with end-to-end latency (sensor-to-operator-to-actuator) not exceeding 150 ms at the 95th percentile under agreed network conditions, enabling safe and responsive remote equipment control.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-008 addresses a critical safety and operational requirement. Teleoperation latency directly impacts operator situational awareness and control responsiveness. The p95 ≤ 150 ms acceptance criterion is a hard constraint for safe remote operation. Refinement clarifies that latency is measured end-to-end (not just network transit), includes both uplink and downlink paths, and is conditioned on agreed network assumptions. High safety criticality demands explicit verification and traceability.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure end-to-end latency under representative network conditions (agreed bandwidth, jitter, packet loss profiles). Conduct operator acceptance testing with simulated remote tasks. Validate p95 latency across 1000+ control cycles under worst-case network conditions within the agreed envelope.
sub_frs: FR-TEL-001: Acquire sensor data (position, orientation, video, proximity) at sufficient frequency to support 150 ms latency budget
FR-TEL-002: Transmit sensor telemetry to remote operator with minimal latency and jitter
FR-TEL-003: Receive operator control commands at remote site with minimal latency
FR-TEL-004: Execute received commands on vehicle actuators with deterministic response time
FR-TEL-005: Monitor and report communication link health and latency metrics
FR-TEL-006: Gracefully degrade or halt operation if latency exceeds safe thresholds

candidate_dps: DP-A: Distributed real-time architecture with edge processing on vehicle, optimized network codec, and operator-side command buffering to minimize round-trip latency
DP-B: Centralized cloud-based teleoperation with edge caching and predictive command interpolation to mask network latency
DP-C: Hybrid architecture with local vehicle autonomy layer handling low-latency control loops, while operator provides high-level tasking over higher-latency link
DP-D: Direct point-to-point communication with hardware-accelerated encoding/decoding and priority queuing to minimize latency variance
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-C: Hybrid architecture with local vehicle autonomy layer handling deterministic low-latency control loops (e.g., stability, collision avoidance), while operator provides high-level tasking and monitoring over the teleoperation link. This decouples safety-critical real-time control from teleoperation latency, allowing the 150 ms constraint to apply to operator command-response cycles rather than all control loops.

potential_sources: Coupling between vehicle autonomy layer (DP-C local control) and teleoperation layer (DP-C remote tasking) must be cleanly decoupled via well-defined autonomy boundaries and mode transitions
Coupling between sensor acquisition frequency and network codec efficiency—higher sensor rates increase bandwidth and latency; codec selection must balance fidelity and latency
Coupling between network link characteristics (agreed conditions) and latency budget allocation—must validate that 150 ms budget is achievable under stated network assumptions
coupling_check.notes: DP-C is preferred because it decouples safety-critical control (handled locally with deterministic latency) from teleoperation latency (which is inherently variable). This reduces coupling between the teleoperation system and the vehicle's core safety functions. Coupling risks are manageable via clear autonomy mode definitions and interface contracts.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Vehicle is equipped with sufficient onboard autonomy (sensors, compute, algorithms) to maintain safe operation during teleoperation link loss or high-latency periods.
Assumption: Operator tasking commands are high-level (e.g., 'move to waypoint', 'lift load') rather than continuous low-level control, allowing 150 ms latency to be acceptable.
Risk: If operator expects direct, low-latency control of all actuators, DP-C may not meet expectations; requires stakeholder alignment on autonomy boundaries.
Risk: Hybrid architecture increases system complexity; autonomy layer must be thoroughly validated to ensure it does not introduce new failure modes.
Risk: Network conditions may vary beyond 'agreed' envelope; system must detect and respond to out-of-spec conditions.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Clarify with stakeholders (Operator, Safety Manager, Tele-operator) the acceptable autonomy boundaries and tasking model. Confirm that high-level tasking with 150 ms latency is operationally acceptable, and that local autonomy will not conflict with operator intent. Document agreed network conditions (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss) explicitly.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the end-to-end latency performance for remote operator command-response cycles. It encompasses sensor data acquisition and transmission (Telemetry interface), operator command reception and transmission (Tasking interface), and latency monitoring. The module interfaces with Vehicle for actuator control and with Safety Services for latency-related safety alerts.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Sensor data (position, orientation, video, proximity, diagnostics) transmitted from vehicle to remote operator. Teleoperation module is responsible for encoding, prioritization, and transmission latency optimization.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Operator control commands (high-level tasking: move, lift, rotate, etc.) received from remote operator and transmitted to vehicle. Teleoperation module is responsible for reception, decoding, and command delivery latency.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Underlying network transport (cellular, satellite, private network) used for bidirectional communication. Teleoperation module manages codec selection, packet prioritization, and latency monitoring over this link.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Alerts to Safety Services when latency exceeds safe thresholds or link health degrades. Enables graceful degradation or operation halt.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the top-level system container. Teleoperation is a major subsystem but not the entire solution. Assigning IM to NGHE Solution would diffuse responsibility across the entire system and obscure ownership of latency performance. Not recommended as the primary IM boundary.

Vehicle module owns actuator control and onboard autonomy. However, teleoperation latency is not solely a vehicle concern—it includes operator-side systems, network codec, and remote command processing. Assigning IM to Vehicle would split latency responsibility between vehicle and operator, creating diffusion. Vehicle is a consumer of Tasking commands, not the owner of teleoperation latency.

Teleoperation module is the natural owner of end-to-end teleoperation latency. It encompasses sensor acquisition, network transmission, command reception, and latency monitoring. Ownership is clear and localized. Interfaces with Vehicle (Tasking output) and Safety Services (SafetyAlert output) are well-defined. High containment, minimal diffusion.

Safety Services owns safety policy enforcement and alerts. Latency monitoring and response is a safety concern, but the primary responsibility for achieving low latency belongs to Teleoperation. Safety Services consumes SafetyAlert from Teleoperation and enforces safe operation policies. Not the primary IM boundary.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the appropriate owner of CR-NGHE-008 (teleoperation latency). It has clear scope boundaries, owns the critical interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, Network Link), and can be held accountable for end-to-end latency performance. Latency monitoring and SafetyAlert generation are contained within Teleoperation, with Safety Services consuming alerts to enforce safe operation policies.
containment_risks: Risk: Teleoperation module may not own the Vehicle's sensor acquisition frequency or actuator response time. Latency budget allocation between Teleoperation and Vehicle must be explicit and validated.

Risk: Network Link interface is external to Teleoperation; network conditions (jitter, packet loss) are outside Teleoperation's direct control. Teleoperation must design for worst-case conditions within the 'agreed' envelope and monitor for out-of-spec conditions.

Risk: Operator-side systems (remote console, command input devices) are not explicitly owned by Teleoperation in this architecture. Operator-side latency must be accounted for in the 150 ms budget; ownership and responsibility must be clarified.
Risk: Graceful degradation and operation halt (FR-TEL-006) require coordination with Safety Services and Vehicle. Teleoperation must interface cleanly with Safety Services to trigger safe shutdown.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Explicitly allocate the 150 ms latency budget across sub-components: sensor acquisition (Vehicle), encoding/transmission (Teleoperation), network transit (Network Link), decoding/command processing (Teleoperation), operator input (external), and actuator response (Vehicle). Document the allocation and validate that each component can meet its target. Clarify ownership of operator-side latency (may be external to NGHE Solution). Define the 'agreed network conditions' and establish monitoring to detect out-of-spec conditions.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-C (hybrid architecture with local autonomy and remote tasking) directly addresses FR-NGHE-008 by decoupling safety-critical control from teleoperation latency. The DP enables the 150 ms constraint to apply to operator command-response cycles, which is operationally meaningful. The DP is feasible and aligns with the high safety criticality of the requirement. No uncoupling or missing DPs identified.

diffusion_risks: Diffusion Risk: Latency budget allocation between Teleoperation and Vehicle is not yet explicit. Teleoperation owns network latency, but Vehicle owns sensor acquisition and actuator response latency. Budget allocation must be documented and validated.

Diffusion Risk: Operator-side latency (remote console, input devices) is not explicitly owned by any NGHE module. If operator-side latency is significant, it must be accounted for in the 150 ms budget or explicitly excluded from NGHE responsibility.

Diffusion Risk: Network Link is external to Teleoperation. Teleoperation must design for worst-case conditions within the 'agreed' envelope, but cannot control network performance. Monitoring and graceful degradation are critical.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module has high containment (0.95) for the DP. Interfaces are well-defined (Telemetry, Tasking, Network Link, SafetyAlert). Ownership is clear. Diffusion risks are manageable via explicit budget allocation and interface contracts. Recommend proceeding to next level with sub-FR decomposition.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of CR-NGHE-008 is feasible via end-to-end latency measurement under representative network conditions. Operator acceptance testing with simulated remote tasks can validate that 150 ms latency is operationally acceptable. Latency monitoring and SafetyAlert generation can be validated via fault injection and network simulation.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation of CR-NGHE-008 to Teleoperation module is clear. Sub-FRs (FR-TEL-001 through FR-TEL-006) are allocated to Teleoperation and Vehicle as appropriate. Latency budget allocation between modules must be documented in the next level of decomposition.

recommended_revisions: Explicitly allocate the 150 ms latency budget across sub-components (sensor acquisition, encoding, network transit, decoding, actuator response). Document the allocation and validate feasibility.
Clarify with stakeholders the acceptable autonomy boundaries and tasking model. Confirm that high-level tasking with 150 ms latency is operationally acceptable.
Define the 'agreed network conditions' (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss) explicitly. Establish monitoring to detect out-of-spec conditions and trigger safe degradation.
Clarify ownership of operator-side latency. If external to NGHE Solution, document the assumption and ensure it is accounted for in the 150 ms budget.
Define the SafetyAlert interface and the response protocol with Safety Services. Specify latency thresholds and safe operation policies.

decision.justification: CR-NGHE-008 is not atomic and requires further decomposition. The refined FR identifies six sub-FRs (FR-TEL-001 through FR-TEL-006) that must be decomposed to allocate latency budget, define interfaces, and assign ownership. The selected DP (hybrid architecture) is sound and addresses the high safety criticality. The assigned IM (Teleoperation) has high containment and clear ownership. Proceeding to the next level of decomposition is necessary to validate latency budget allocation, clarify interface contracts, and ensure all sub-FRs are addressed.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-TEL-001: Acquire sensor data (position, orientation, video, proximity) at sufficient frequency to support 150 ms latency budget
FR-TEL-002: Transmit sensor telemetry to remote operator with minimal latency and jitter
FR-TEL-003: Receive operator control commands at remote site with minimal latency
FR-TEL-004: Execute received commands on vehicle actuators with deterministic response time
FR-TEL-005: Monitor and report communication link health and latency metrics
FR-TEL-006: Gracefully degrade or halt operation if latency exceeds safe thresholds

assumptions: Vehicle is equipped with sufficient onboard autonomy (sensors, compute, algorithms) to maintain safe operation during teleoperation link loss or high-latency periods.
Operator tasking commands are high-level (e.g., 'move to waypoint', 'lift load') rather than continuous low-level control, allowing 150 ms latency to be acceptable.
Network conditions are within the 'agreed' envelope (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss). Out-of-spec conditions will be detected and trigger safe degradation.
Operator-side latency (remote console, input devices) is either negligible or explicitly excluded from the 150 ms NGHE budget.
Safety Services will enforce safe operation policies based on SafetyAlert signals from Teleoperation.
open_risks: Latency budget allocation between Teleoperation and Vehicle is not yet explicit. Risk of over-allocation or under-allocation.
Hybrid architecture increases system complexity. Risk of autonomy layer introducing new failure modes or conflicting with operator intent.
Network conditions may vary beyond 'agreed' envelope. Risk of latency exceeding safe thresholds without detection.
Operator acceptance of high-level tasking model with 150 ms latency is not yet validated. Risk of operational mismatch.
SafetyAlert interface and response protocol with Safety Services is not yet defined. Risk of unsafe degradation or operation halt.
evidence_needed: Latency budget allocation across sensor acquisition, encoding, network transit, decoding, and actuator response. Validation that each component can meet its target.
Definition of 'agreed network conditions' (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss). Justification that 150 ms is achievable under these conditions.
Operator acceptance testing with simulated remote tasks. Validation that high-level tasking with 150 ms latency is operationally acceptable.
Autonomy layer design and validation. Specification of autonomy boundaries and mode transitions. Verification that autonomy does not conflict with operator intent.
SafetyAlert interface definition and response protocol with Safety Services. Specification of latency thresholds and safe operation policies.
Network monitoring and graceful degradation design. Specification of out-of-spec detection and safe shutdown procedures.
